impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products allegedly lack substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld that whether the tied products had substantial non-infringing uses was a jury question, and the jury found against Ingevity.
impacts.positiveImplications: Confirms that companies accused of patent-based tying can still argue their products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses as a defense to antitrust liability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle patent licenses with requirements to purchase unpatented products face significant antitrust exposure. Jury findings on substantial non-infringing uses can sustain tying liability even where the patent holder disputes the characterization.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model of function calls are abstract and ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101, and whether damages for foreign sales of infringing software are recoverable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patent claims were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. The ruling significantly narrows the scope of patentable subject matter for software-based anomaly detection methods.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing patent infringement suits involving software detection methods gain a stronger basis for § 101 eligibility challenges. The foreign sales damages ruling limits plaintiffs' ability to recover damages for overseas revenue.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity and software anomaly detection space face heightened invalidity risk under § 101. Universities and research institutions monetizing software patents may see reduced damages potential.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content presentation specifications to a wireless device's capabilities before delivering requested content are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming server-side content tailoring for wireless device capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing a $2.5 million jury verdict in GoTV's favor on the '715 patent and entering judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to abstract ideas of adapting content to device capabilities.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and content delivery companies gain significant protection against patent assertions covering basic server-side content adaptation to device capabilities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims covering content delivery optimization and device-adaptive streaming face increased invalidity risk under § 101. The decision further narrows patentable subject matter for internet content delivery technologies.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was properly granted on inequitable conduct in patent procurement and on a Walker Process fraud claim alleging attempted monopolization through enforcement of a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact. Tenaris obtained patents on coiled tubing technology after acquiring prior art documents (CYMAX Documents) that it allegedly failed to disclose during prosecution. The court held that the district court improperly resolved factual disputes at summary judgment.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct retain the ability to contest intent and materiality findings at trial rather than having them resolved on summary judgment.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire prior art through corporate acquisitions face heightened scrutiny regarding disclosure obligations during prosecution. Walker Process antitrust claims based on fraudulently obtained patents remain viable where factual disputes exist about intent to deceive the PTO.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is compatible with the governing statute and whether the common law right of public access to judicial records applies.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute and that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial records. This requires the ITC to evaluate confidentiality on a case-by-case basis.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, competitors, and the public gain greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings. Companies participating in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations may benefit from access to non-confidential business information of competitors.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses in trade investigations face increased risk of business information becoming publicly accessible. The ITC must now individually assess confidentiality rather than relying on blanket designations, potentially increasing administrative burden and litigation over disclosure.

impacts.caseName: Genuine Enabling Technology LLC v. Sony Group Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's failure to account for all elements of the corresponding structure disclosed in the specification for a means-plus-function limitation defeats an infringement case at summary judgment.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of noninfringement for Sony, holding that GET's infringement analysis was deficient because it failed to account for many elements of the corresponding structure for the 'encoding means' limitation in its patent. The court emphasized that means-plus-function infringement requires matching all disclosed structural elements.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers benefit from strict application of means-plus-function analysis, requiring patent holders to demonstrate that all corresponding structural elements are present in accused products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting means-plus-function claims face an exacting burden to map every element of the disclosed corresponding structure to accused products, or risk summary judgment of noninfringement.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims reciting means-plus-function 'control means' for a crop harvester header height control system are invalid as indefinite when the specification describes the corresponding structure as 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that asserted claims of the '395 patent were indefinite, holding that the specification adequately disclosed the corresponding structure for the 'control means' limitation. The court remanded for further proceedings on infringement against Deere.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders can rely on specification descriptions of 'conventional' components as sufficient corresponding structure for means-plus-function claims, preserving claim validity.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face reduced ability to invalidate means-plus-function claims as indefinite when the specification provides some structural disclosure, even if described generically as conventional.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent damages expert's report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft. The court found the district court improperly excluded the expert's opinions on the financial benefits of Microsoft's alleged infringement of patents related to virtual network optimization in Azure's SmartNICs and VFP technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs gain greater latitude in presenting damages expert testimony, with appellate courts willing to reverse overly aggressive Daubert exclusions that effectively eliminate a party's remedy.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent suits face reduced ability to use Daubert motions as a dispositive mechanism to eliminate damages cases entirely at the summary judgment stage.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding patent infringement experts' testimony and granting JMOL of no induced infringement of spinal surgery device patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and JMOL for DePuy in a case involving patents on spinal derotation surgical techniques. The court held the district court improperly excluded technical and infringement expert testimony, warranting a new trial where both experts may testify.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device space retain the ability to present expert testimony on induced infringement, with appellate courts scrutinizing trial court expert exclusions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face continued exposure to induced infringement claims when their products can be configured by surgeons to practice patented surgical methods.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions on IPR discretionary denials are general policy statements exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued discretion to deny IPR petitions without being constrained by formal rulemaking procedures, maintaining flexibility in patent validity challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners lose their challenge to the PTO's discretionary denial framework, meaning potentially meritorious patent challenges may continue to be denied institution without formal procedural safeguards.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly applied 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1) to issue a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents while finding no violation by active respondents on trademark infringement and dilution claims.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's appeal of the no-violation finding against active respondents as untimely but affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under Section 337(g)(1). The court confirmed that the ITC properly presumed complaint allegations as true against defaulting parties and issued an LEO without requiring Crocs to present separate infringement contentions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Trademark holders can obtain limited exclusion orders against defaulting respondents in Section 337 proceedings based on presumed facts without needing to independently prove infringement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark holders must strictly adhere to appeal deadlines for ITC determinations, as untimely appeals will be dismissed regardless of the merits of the underlying trademark claims.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a mandatory obligation for the SBA to award grants to eligible applicants in the order applications were received, giving rise to Tucker Act jurisdiction for damages claims.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed Tucker Act jurisdiction over claims by over 300 restaurants that applied for RRF grants but were not funded despite applying before other recipients. The court held the statute's 'shall award grants' language created a mandatory payment obligation, and the SBA's failure to process applications in order gave rise to cognizable damages claims against the government.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses denied congressionally mandated grants or benefits due to agency processing errors have a viable path to recover damages under the Tucker Act when statutes use mandatory language.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potential liability for hundreds of unfunded RRF applications, establishing precedent that mandatory statutory language in grant programs can create enforceable payment obligations.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities enabling asynchronous viewing of multimedia data streams are ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal for failure to state a claim, holding that the web conferencing patent claims were ineligible under § 101. The claims covered systems allowing participants to view data streams asynchronously during or after a conferencing session, which the court found directed to abstract ideas.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies offering web conferencing and collaboration tools gain further protection against patent assertions covering basic time-shifting and asynchronous viewing functionality.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims covering web conferencing features face continued narrowing of patent eligibility for software-based collaboration tools.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly denied JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages for a patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical and electrical trunk connections.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL on obviousness and new trial on damages, upholding the jury's verdict that Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial tree trunk connectors was not obvious. The decision sustains patent protection for innovative consumer product designs in the seasonal goods market.
impacts.positiveImplications: Consumer product patent holders benefit from deference to jury verdicts on non-obviousness, particularly where the patented invention combines known elements in a novel integrated design.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the consumer products space face sustained enforcement risk from design integration patents where obviousness challenges are rejected at trial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether registered nurses employed by the Indian Health Service under Title 5 are entitled to enhanced compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38 provisions applicable to eligible healthcare employees.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Court of Federal Claims' dismissal of claims by IHS nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work during the COVID-19 pandemic. The court held that the nurses adequately stated claims under Title 38's compensation provisions and Title 5 regulations, remanding for further proceedings on a potential class action.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potential back-pay liability for nurses and other healthcare workers who were denied enhanced compensation rates during the pandemic. The ruling could affect compensation practices for Title 5 healthcare employees government-wide.
